titleStmt.title: Letter from Cis M. Coyle to James Ryan, 11 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Cis M. Coyle

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Cis M Coyle to James Ryan (1891-1970) on 11 July 1916. This letter was written while Ryan was a prisoner in Frongach in Wales. She refers mainly to mutual friends at home and fellow prisoners of Ryan's.James Ryan studied medicine in UCD. He was appointed chief medical officer in the GPO during the Easter Rising. After the Rising he was imprisoned in Stafford and, later, Frongoch and was released in August 1916. Ryan was later a founder member of Fianna Fáil and a government minister.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
58 Aughrim St July 11th. 1916 Tuesday My dear Jim I hope you are
very well and that
you like the change
to Camps. Did you
see the Councillor at
all since he went
to Frongoch? I suppose
there are a great
number of you in Camp.
Are you all to-gether? Liam has not written
to me since he went
to Wales. I hope he is
not ill. Kathleen
Morrin is with me
to-day. She came up
to see a Dr with her
arm. She got a bad
fall off her bicycle
She says to tell you
she is sorry you are
not here to cure
it for her. All friends
wish to be remembered
to you. How is
Michael Kelly? Tell
him I was enquiring
for him and also
tell all my other
friends in exile.
I believe a lot of the
Irish prisoners have
been tried, have you
been before the Advisory
Committee yet? Is there
any chance of your
getting home soon
I wonder. Good bye
for the present with
kindest regards from
all. Very Sincerely Yours Cis M Coyle
